For enquiries about this publication, please contact: James Niven, Health and Social Care Analysis, Scottish Government. james.niven@gov.scot 5 Executive Summary  Currently Scotland operates an opt in system of organ and tissue donation. This review was undertaken to provide an overview of evidence regarding opt out systems of donation and to inform the development and implementation of a workable opt out system of organ and tissue donation  The majority of the Scottish population is likely to support opt out legislation for organ donation (59% supported the principle of opt out in a 2016 TNS survey1) Although, there is little firm evidence that opt out legislation in isolation causes increases in organ donation and transplantation, there is encouraging evidence that, as part of a package of measures, opt out legislation can lead to increases in organ donation and transplantation. - A body of evidence shows that there is a well-established association between higher deceased donation/transplantation and opt out legislation/systems compared to opt in. Although it is important to note that not all opt out countries have higher rates of deceased donation and transplantation - A range of before and after studies suggest a change to a system of opt out donation can result in increases in organ donation, although this evidence base is somewhat limited - A small body of evidence from psychological experiments explains how opt out could increase donation and transplant, although its real word applicability is limited - It is challenging to separate the impact of opt out legislation from other concurrent system changes, thereby limiting the evidence base  There is some encouraging but limited evidence that opt out systems can bring about wider positive changes, which are likely to increase organ donation and transplantation - There is a small body of international evidence that suggests opt out is associated with increased willingness to donate. People in opt out countries were between 17–29 per cent more likely to report willingness to donate their own organs and 27–56 per cent more likely to authorise the donation of their own relatives’ organs, compared to respondents living in opt in countries 1 TNS Organ Donation 2016 Campaign Evaluation (September 2016, unpublished) 6 - There is a limited body of evidence with regards to the impact of opt out legislation and systems on the number of people registering decisions on a register (where they are used). - Existing evidence draws no firm conclusions about the impact of opt in legislation on family authorisation. A small number of studies suggest how family authorisation could increase as a result of opt out. However, there is also evidence (e.g. from Chile) indicating decreases in family authorisation - Welsh evidence suggests that opt out is likely to increase awareness of organ donation more widely, although only limited conclusions can be drawn from this  There is a strong body of evidence that highlights the importance of non legislative measures, such as improving infrastructure. This suggests that opt out systems and legislation function most effectively as part of a wider package of measures, - Public awareness of opt out appears to be important and associated with increased willingness to donate, particularly in opt out systems - Public attitudes are likely to be crucial. There are several country based case studies (e.g. Brazil) which indicate that negative public attitudes, particularly due to medical mistrust, can pose a risk when implementing opt out. There is strong evidence that families should be encouraged to have conversations about organ donation and where this happens it is likely to increase organ donation and transplantation. It is vital that families are aware of what organ donation is, when it can occur and their involvement in the process - There is evidence which indicates that improving health care staff awareness, knowledge and confidence in relation to donation is likely to be important in raising organ donor numbers  There are various contextual factors (e.g. mortality rates) that are likely to influence the impact of opt out and these should be considered when comparing countries  Religion and ethnicity are likely to be important and the relationship with organ donation is complex, but there was little evidence identified with regards to opt out on specific religious or ethnic groups. 7 1. Introduction 1.1 Context At present, in Scotland, in order to become an organ or tissue donor after death, a person must either have expressed a wish/decision to do so or one of their relatives can authorise donation on their behalf. This system is known as an 'opt in' system. In many cases, individuals make their decision known by joining the NHS Organ Donor Register (ODR). If an individual has not given authorisation and they could be a potential organ or tissue donor, their nearest relative will be asked to make a decision. However, a nearest relative cannot authorise donation if they know that their relative did not want to donate. Having such sensitive conversations with families at the time an individual dies is very difficult, and, understandably, many families find it impossible to consider such requests when they are often in shock or grieving. In such circumstances a significant minority of families (43.6% in 2017-18) do not give authorisation for donation for transplantation, although survey evidence suggests that the majority of people in Scotland support donation. Conversely opt out systems presume (or deem) that a person consents to becoming a deceased donor if they haven’t actively opted out of donating e.g. by joining an opt out register or otherwise making their decision clear. In a hard opt out system, family members are generally given little if any input to the decision, but most opt out systems have some form of safeguards to allow families to say if they don’t think their relative wished to donate – this is known as a ‘soft’ opt out system. A number of soft opt out systems also exclude certain categories of people, such as children, from being covered by the presumed consent arrangements. In Wales, a soft opt out system of deceased organ and tissue donation was implemented in December 2015. This continues to allow for potential donors to provide consent themselves, for example by joining the ODR or for a relative to provide consent on their behalf. It also provides for a statutory ‘opt out’ ODR of people who do not want to donate their organs or tissue. Where an individual has neither opted in nor opted out then their consent can normally be deemed. However, this is subject to certain safeguards, for example checking that family members are not aware of the potential donor having said to anyone that they did not want to be a donor. In addition, children, adults with long-term incapacity and people ordinarily resident in Wales for less than twelve months cannot have their consent deemed. In England and Northern Ireland, the existing legislative system under the Human Tissue Act 2004 is an opt in system, which is very similar to that in Scotland. However, the UK Government has indicated that it is supportive of proposals to move to an opt out system for England and is expected to publish its response to the recent consultation on opt out shortly. This means that an opt out system may also be implemented in England in the future. There are no current known plans to move to an opt out system in Northern Ireland. 8 In Scotland, since 2013/2014 there has been an 18% increase in people registering a decision to become a donor after death and the percentage of people on the ODR in Scotland is the highest in the UK (48% as at June 20182) (NHS Blood and Transplant , 2017). Survey evidence3 suggests that the Scottish public are generally supportive of the principle of opt out. Evidence from the wider UK suggests that support for opt out has been growing over time and is now supported by the majority of the population (Rithalia, et al., 2009). This is further discussed in section 4.6. This review was undertaken to provide an overview of evidence to inform decision making concerning opt out and its potential implementation. 1.2 Aims The overarching aim of this rapid evidence review is to explore the evidence of effectiveness of opt out organ donation legislation and systems4. Specifically it aims to examine evidence regarding:  the effectiveness of opt out legislation and systems in increasing the number of transplants and donations  contextual factors that are likely to influence organ donation and transplantation  other non-legislative factors that are positively associated with successful opt out systems and higher rates of organ donation independently of opt out (infrastructure, public awareness/attitudes, family authorisation, staff attitudes and awareness, media campaigns and support for organ donation)  the potential influence religion and ethnicity could have on opt out. There is a particular focus on evidence from Wales because it is more comparable than many other countries that have adopted opt out legislation and systems. 2 A backlog of registration activity made via GP Services is not accounted for 3 The survey asked “How much do you agree or disagree that everyone should be presumed to be willing to be an organ donor unless they register a wish otherwise” - 59% agreed with this. It was carried out by TNS but has not been published. 4 Legislation refers solely to the change in law and presumption that those who do not declare their wishes are deemed to want to donate. The term system is not particularly well defined in the literature, but in this context it refers to the legislation change and the wider media and awareness raising campaigns and staff training that often occur concurrently with the legislation change. 9 1.1 Methodology Search strategy A literature search was conducted by the Scottish Government Library and covered a wide range of resources, including: IDOX; K&E; Web of Science; and ProQuest Databases. The majority of the literature was published within the last few years. Both peer reviewed and non-peer reviewed (grey literature) papers were considered. This was partly due to limited evidence base regarding the impact of opt out. This paper considers much of the research covered in a systematic review for the Welsh Government and also more recent evidence, such as international comparative study by Shepherd et al (Shepherd, et al., 2014). Peer review This paper was peer reviewed by researchers external to Scottish Government at NHS Health Scotland. Limitations The review was completed in a short timescale, and consequently it is not intended as an exhaustive critical appraisal of the research evidence. There are limitations to the current evidence base which are highlighted throughout. For instance, many international studies do not distinguish between hard and soft opt out systems and their impacts despite the substantial differences in the importance of family views/consent. This limits the extent to which we can generalise these findings to the potential implementation of opt out in Scotland. In addition, there was often a lack of specific evidence with regards to opt out. In this case, inferences have been made from the wider organ donation literature. In the literature that examined the influence of other factors in increasing donation and transplantation, it was often unclear what the most crucial factors were. A lack of evidence was identified with regards to tissue donation and transplantation and therefore it has not been considered specifically in this review. 10 2. Effectiveness of opt out legislation and systems This section reviews the evidence on the effectiveness of opt out organ donation legislation and systems. For the purpose of this review, factors associated with effectiveness of opt out systems and legislation includes the number of organs donated and transplanted. Wider effectiveness factors also include changes in attitude (such as being more supportive about donation and behaviours such as greater discussion with families) and awareness/understanding concerning donation. Registration to be an organ donor is considered, as it remains important because of its association with increased authorisation for donation. It is not treated as a key measure of effectiveness, as in an opt out system not registering a decision is also considered an active decision to donate. Generally the ultimate aim of organ donation systems is to maximise the number of successful donations to satisfy demand for organs. Increasing awareness of organ donation is associated with increasing numbers of people registering to donate their organs, which is in turn associated with increasing the number of organ transplants. However, increasing awareness and the number of people on a register (for those countries which have one) does not guarantee an increase in the number of transplants. For instance, any increase in the number of people registering as donors is likely to be followed by a significant time lag in donation increases (as those who are newly registering are unlikely to die in the near future). In addition, in many cases those registering may not represent proportionately those who are most likely ultimately to be able to potentially donate. This is as those on registers tend (based on evidence from the UK) to be higher socio economic groups and (in Scotland) particularly in the 30 to 49 years age groups5 (Optimisa Research, 2014), and do not necessarily mirror proportionately the population who tend to be potential deceased donors. Another challenge to consider is that the context in which people can donate organs, as deceased donors, is relatively small as only 1% of people die in circumstances which would make them eligible to donate. It is clear that more people on a register does not automatically result in more donors. Unless figures are examined over long periods of time, this makes it difficult to robustly conclude that any change is as a result of a policy intervention. 2.1 Comparison studies between opt in and opt out countries in relation to donation and transplantation This section reviews the body of evidence examining the differences between opt in and opt out countries, in relation to willingness to donate, family authorisation, deceased and living donation. 5 As well as more likely to be disproportionately White 11 Opt out organ donation impact on willingness to donate and family authorisation (or willingness to allow donation to proceed) We explore the association between opt out systems of organ donation on willingness to donate, as this is an important step towards donation. Mossialos et al examined individuals’ willingness to donate using survey data from 15 European countries. The study found that those in opt out countries were between 17–29 per cent more likely to report willingness to donate their own organs and 27–56 per cent more likely to authorise the donation of their own relatives’ organs compared to respondents living in opt in countries (Mossialos, 2008). Shepherd et al found that family members were much more likely to be willing to authorise the donation of their loved one’s organs when they had registered to be a donor themselves (Shepherd & O'Carroll, 2014). The literature regarding family authorisation and opt out could be further developed as only one study was identified. The evidence indicates there is an association between opt out and higher willingness to donate (Mossialos, 2008). A potential causal mechanism for this is discussed in the experimental studies literature (discussed below). Deceased and living donation and the number of transplants Deceased donation generally represents the largest source of organs and therefore is particularly important to consider. This section examines the association between opt out and deceased donation/the number of transplants. Shepherd et al (Shepherd; O'Carroll and Ferguson, 2014) conducted a statistical analysis comparing opt in and out countries in terms of both deceased and living donation. The evidence suggests that deceased donor rates (per million population) were higher in opt out than opt in consent countries. However, they found that living donation, which generally represents a smaller source of transplants, was lower in opt out countries. Further evidence to support this was identified by (Bendorf, et al., 2013), who found lower rates of living donation for kidney transplantation in opt out countries. Despite this association, there was limited consideration in the existing literature as to a potential cause and this would benefit from further exploration (Ugar, 2015). Bendorf (2013) identified that the number of kidneys and livers transplanted from deceased donors was higher in opt out systems, despite lower living donation. Adadie et al examined data from 27 EU countries and found that opt out countries have higher deceased (cadaveric) donation (28% to 32% higher) and kidney transplants (27% to 31%), after controlling for a wide range of factors. A Welsh Government systematic review compared the number of deceased donors per million population in 2011 across countries (The Welsh Government, 2012). The countries with the smallest number of deceased donors were Bulgaria, Turkey, Cyprus and Greece, all of which had an opt out (presumed consent) organ donation system at the time of the study. However the countries with the greatest number of deceased donors per million population also have opt out systems, such as Portugal, Belgium, Croatia and Spain (The Welsh Government, 2012). Countries with opt out systems therefore did not automatically have high rates of donation. 12 This suggests that opt out systems are not a guarantee of success and highlights the importance of considering opt out as part of wider package of measures/initiatives. Overall, despite these exceptions, there is a well-established association between opt out and higher rates of deceased donation across a body of evidence (Abadie, A, Gay, S, 2004) (Ugar, 2015) (Shepherd; O'Carroll and Ferguson, 2014) (Bilgel, 2013). There is also some evidence of an association of greater willingness to donate and higher levels of family authorisation of donation. However this is not sufficient to establish a casual impact. 2.2 Evidence from before and after studies A range of studies have examined countries before and after the implementation of opt out and these further support the association between opt out and higher donation rates and explore a potential causal impact (The Welsh Government, 2012). Whilst the studies have become increasingly sophisticated at controlling for a wide range of factors that influence organ donation rates (see for example, Shepherd and O’Carroll, 2014), a limitation of the current evidence base is that legislation changes occur concurrently with other factors associated with opt out legislation, such as increased media campaigning and public awareness. The vast majority of the studies make little effort to investigate the impact of any other changes taking place simultaneously. It is therefore nearly impossible to ascertain if it was the legislation or the changes to the system associated with opt out that had the greatest impact. Impact on proportion registering to be an organ donor Registration appears to be an important factor in obtaining authorisation. The Organ Donation Taskforce 2008 report found that 90% of families in the UK allowed donation to proceed when a potential donor had joined the ODR compared to 60% when they had not (Organ Donation Taskforce, 2008). The complexity of this relationship is discussed further in section 4.9. This review could not identify any studies examining the impact before and after the introduction of opt out legislation on the numbers of people registering. However, evidence from the Welsh impact evaluation of opt out is encouraging. This found there has been an increase in those registering on the NHS ODR- 34% of the Welsh population were on the ODR in 2014/2015, compared to 36% in 2015/16 (at the time of implementation of opt out) and 38% in 2017/18. 13 International evidence that examines the impact on donation and transplant numbers There is a range of evidence which examines the impact of opt out on organ donation and transplant numbers before and after implementation. However, as some studies measure increases in organ donation using different organ types, comparison between studies is difficult. Rithalia et al’s (2009) systematic review examined five before and after studies from three different countries (Austria, Belgium and Singapore), and concluded that there was an increase in deceased donation in all of these studies. The Belgian study is the most relevant as it is the only one where a soft opt out system applies. However, it is limited by the length of time that has passed since the introduction of opt out in 1986. The study identified that kidney transplantation from both deceased and living donors increased from 18.9 to 41.3 per million population per year over a three year period after the change in legislation (Roels, 1991). Austria and Singapore both have hard opt out systems, where family members do not have a say about the use of a loved one’s organs. Comparisons to soft opt out are limited due to the significant differences in authorisation between hard and soft opt out. The Singapore study found that kidney donation increased from 4.7 to 31.3 per million population in the three year period after a change in legislation (Rithalia, et al., 2009). In Austria there was evidence of an increase of 4.6 to 10.1 donors per million population per year in the four years after the introduction of presumed consent. The largest increase in Austria was 27.2 donors per million population (pmp) in the five years after the introduction of infrastructure changes (such as full time transplant coordinators). The study therefore supports the premise that a change to hard opt out legislation can increase organ donation, but continues to support the evidence base that other factors are important. There are limitations generalising these findings to soft opt out legislation. In the international evidence base, there is variation in the size of increase in deceased donation rates. For instance, some studies report opt out legislation is associated with increases in deceased organ donation rates of 13–18 % (Bilgel, 2013) and 25–30% (Abadie, A, Gay, S, 2004). Overall, these studies support the notion that changes in systems to an opt out system increase the number of people registering a decision and making people more aware of organ donation policy (this is further supported in the evidence from Wales, discussed below). It is important to note that the literature did not distinguish if this difference was due to the availability of an organ donor register or awareness of organ donation as a whole. There is also firm evidence of an association between opt out and higher deceased donation rates. However, this evidence is insufficient to conclude that opt out legislation in isolation causes higher donation rates. As discussed above, these studies are limited by the concurrent changes that occur to support opt out legislation as part of a wider opt out system. In addition, further research is required to examine the specific impact of soft opt out, as only 14 one study from the international literature was specifically identified which examined this area. In summary, it is likely that opt out systems and the often concurrent changes are likely to contribute to increases in organ donation. It is challenging to establish if the legislation in isolation leads to increases from the current evidence base, partially because of the other changes that occur concurrently. 2.3 Welsh impact evaluation Whilst there are key differences (donation rates, proportion of the population on the ODR) between organ donation in Wales and Scotland, the similar context6 makes it a useful example to examine in detail. The Welsh impact evaluation (The Welsh Government, 2017) examined the impact of opt out legislation and associated system changes three years after implementation. Due to this short timescale, it is challenging to draw any firm conclusions on the overall impact of opt out, but there are some clear changes already apparent. Public awareness Overall there was increased awareness of the change to the opt out policy among both NHS staff and the general public. For the public, this has been associated with increased support for opt out. However, awareness had recently decreased, highlighting the importance of maintaining continuous awareness campaigns when implementing opt out. Whilst this increased awareness is encouraging, it is impossible to tell if this change is a result of opt out legislation or concurrent changes. In addition, it is challenging to establish the long term impact of the opt out system because the evaluation was undertaken only a short time after the implementation of the legislation. Organ Donor Register (ODR) The increased publicity campaigns associated with opt out in Wales are likely to have contributed to the number of people opting out on the ODR (it was possible to opt out on the ODR from six months before the introduction of opt out in Wales). --- SOURCE 2: Analysis of Attitudes Towards Opt-Out Organ Donation Consent ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0041134524005268

SUMMARY:
Analysis of Attitudes Towards Opt-Out Organ Donation Consent: A Cross-Sectional Study Among Saudi Arabian Residents - ScienceDirect The agreement with opt-out consent for organ donation was explored by asking the following question “Saudi as well as non-Saudi residents should be automatically included on the Organ Donor register of Saudi Arabia, with the ability to refuse if they wish” and responses were collected using a five-point scale, ranging from "strongly agree" to "strongly disagree," to gauge participants' agreement with the statement. Out of the 619 participants who supported the opt-out consent system, 576 individuals (93.05%) expressed agreement with promoting organ donation. The study revealed a substantial agreement within the Saudi population favoring opt-out consent for organ donation, with 44.4% supporting the automatic enrollment of both Saudi and non-Saudi residents in the donor registry, with the choice to decline participation.

ABSTRACT
--------

### Objective

The study aims to investigate public opinion on opt-out organ donation registration in Saudi Arabia, addressing a gap identified in existing research that reveals varied attitudes and intentions among the population, as indicated by studies in Qatar and Saudi Arabia.

### Methods

This study employed a secondary analysis approach, utilizing data from a cross-sectional survey conducted online among 1397 residents of Saudi Arabia. The survey utilized a GoogleTM form questionnaire adapted from a previous study in Qatar. The questionnaire comprised three sections, gathering socio-demographic information, assessing general awareness about organ donation, and exploring participants' agreement with opt-out consent and beliefs related to organ donation using the Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) model.

### Results

Among the participants, 44.4% supported opt-out consent, with 25.7% females and 39.1% Saudi citizens. Females and diploma/graduation-level education were significantly associated with opt-out support (_P_< .001, _P_ = .012, respectively). 98.06% of opt-out supporters were familiar with organ donation. 93.05% agreed to promote organ donation, 98.38% believed registration saves lives, and 81.91% were willing if family had no objections. 86.75% were ready with more information, and 85.78% if informed about their religion's perspective. 92.25% believed living and posthumous donation positively impact life after death. Concerns included inadequate care (33.44%) and bodily disfigurement (28.43%) postmortem. Majority felt healthy (45.56%) and appropriate in age for donation (57.67%). Among opt-out supporters, 20.84% expressed interest, 8.4% conveyed disinterest, while 36.34% remained undecided regarding organ donation registration.

### Conclusion

The study reveals a considerable openness among Saudis toward adopting an opt-out organ donation system, suggesting a potential avenue for increased organ donation rates. While acknowledging cultural nuances, particularly familial influences, targeted interventions are vital to overcome specific barriers and ensure the successful implementation of an opt-out policy.

Introduction
------------

The increasing gap between organ demand and donor availability has prompted global efforts to address this issue [1]. Low consent rates remain a significant barrier to organ donation, alongside other challenges like limited understanding, religious beliefs, and racial factors [2]. To boost deceased organ donation rates, many countries are adopting innovative approaches such as the opt-out consent system, where individuals are considered donors unless they explicitly decline [3]. The success of this system in Spain has led several countries like Wales, the Netherlands, England, and Scotland to transition from opt-in to opt-out systems [3,4]. Legal support for presumed consent has proven effective in simplifying the process, aligning with the default choice, and translating intentions into actual organ donations when compared to opt-in systems [3,5].

Another critical issue in organ donation involves the balance between an individual's posthumous donation rights and the family's decision-making authority, regardless of the deceased's prior intentions [6]. Recent systematic reviews on public attitudes toward the role of families in deceased organ donation decision-making reveal that most individuals support the family's role as a surrogate decision-maker when the deceased has not expressed a preference. However, responses differ when the deceased provided explicit consent, depending on whether respondents view themselves as potential donors or the deceased's next-of-kin [7]. While patient autonomy is highly valued, many countries permit families to override the deceased's wishes [8]. The family's role in honoring the deceased's wishes and the factors influencing their decisions significantly impact organ donation rates [9]. Concerns have also arisen about whether presumed consent accurately reflects the patient's wishes, potentially compromising donor autonomy if individuals did not want to donate but failed to opt-out [3].

Although limited studies explore public opinion on opt-out systems in the Arab world, recent research in Qatar and Saudi Arabia indicates varying attitudes and intentions. For instance, a study in Qatar found that most participants expressed a positive attitude but lacked knowledge, behavioral beliefs, and intentions regarding opt-out systems [10]. Similarly, a study in Saudi Arabia reported that only 19.6% were willing to register as organ/tissue donors, regardless of their trust in the country's medical facilities [11]. To address this gap, we conducted a study to assess the Saudi population's public opinion on opt-out organ donation registration within Saudi Arabia.

Methodology: The data utilized in this study is a subset derived from an online cross-sectional survey exploring attitudes and behaviors towards organ donation in Saudi Arabia. The survey was conducted among 1397 residents of Saudi Arabia and was carried out between June 2022-Dec 2022.

The current investigation adopted an online cross-sectional survey methodology using a GoogleTM form questionnaire. Approval for the study protocol was granted by the institutional ethical committee under approval code UJ-REC-234. The survey instrument was adapted from a prior study examining beliefs and intentions related to organ donation in Qatar [12]. Participants were given a clear overview of the study's purpose and were required to explicitly agree to participate before proceeding to the questionnaire.

The questionnaire encompassed three main sections: the first section focused on gathering socio-demographic information, including age, gender, nationality, religion, marital status, education, occupation, average monthly income, and location. The second part aimed to assess general awareness regarding organ donation. The agreement with opt-out consent for organ donation was explored by asking the following question “Saudi as well as non-Saudi residents should be automatically included on the Organ Donor register of Saudi Arabia, with the ability to refuse if they wish” and responses were collected using a five-point scale, ranging from "strongly agree" to "strongly disagree," to gauge participants' agreement with the statement. The final segment of the questionnaire was centered on the Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) model. It aimed to explore participants' normative, behavioral, and control beliefs pertaining to organ donation by incorporating TPB constructs. Responses were collected using a five-point scale, ranging from "strongly agree" to "strongly disagree," to gauge participants' agreement with provided statements.

The data analysis was performed using the Statistical Package for Social Sciences (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA, version 26.0 for Windows). Descriptive statistics were employed to analyze the socio-demographic variables. Additionally, chi-square tests were utilized to examine the relationship between agreement with opt-out consent for organ donation, sociodemographic variables, and beliefs.

Results: Among the 1397 participants surveyed, 619 individuals (44.4%) expressed agreement with an opt-out consent system. Among these, 358 (25.7%) were female, 546 (39.1%) were Saudi citizens, 320 (22.9%) were married, and 412 (29.5%) held a Diploma/graduation degree. Notably, 98.2% of respondents identified as Muslims. Additionally, 43.8% were unemployed during the study period, while 42% reported a monthly income of less than 5000 Saudi Riyals. The majority of responses originated from the western region (65.5%), followed by the central region (14%).

Significantly, there was an observed correlation between agreement with the opt-out consent system and gender (_P_< .001) and education level (_P_ = .012), with a higher proportion of females and those educated to a diploma/graduation level showing more inclination towards the opt-out consent system. Further insights into the demographic and socio-economic traits of participants opting for either an opt-in or opt-out consent system can be found in Table 1.

Among the 619 participants (44.4%) who supported an opt-out consent system, a significant majority of 607 individuals (98.06%) were familiar with the term "organ donation." Surprisingly, only 71 participants (11.47%) had ever attended an organ donation promotion campaign.

Regarding organ donation specifics, 362 participants (58.48%) knew that organ donation registration could commence at 18 years and above, while 90 individuals (14.54%) believed organs could be donated at any age. When asked about their understanding of death, 197 participants (31.83%) expressed that it signified no heartbeat and no breath, whereas a higher number, 340 individuals (54.93%), understood death as brain death with the heart beating on a ventilator.

Concerning religious perspectives, 493 participants (79.64%) knew that their religion permitted organ donation. Surprisingly, only 249 participants (40.23%) personally knew someone who had donated organs.

Regarding specific organ donation knowledge, 565 participants (91.28%) were aware that part of the liver could be donated to relatives during their lifetime, with only 41 participants (6.62%) believing this involved health risks. Additionally, 548 participants (88.53%) knew that one of their two kidneys could be donated, and 389 individuals (62.84%) understood that it was a safe procedure.

Participants also demonstrated awareness about Saudi Arabia's organ donation policies: 550 individuals (88.85%) knew that the policy prohibits buying and selling of organs, while 536 participants (86.59%) were aware that it provides equal access to transplant facilities for all Saudi residents. Moreover, 549 participants (88.69%) knew that donated organs from deceased donors were given to the first person on the waiting list. Last, a vast majority, 597 participants (96.45%), were aware that the donor's family is never pressured for donation.

Out of the 619 participants who supported the opt-out consent system, 576 individuals (93.05%) expressed agreement with promoting organ donation. Similarly, 609 participants (98.38%) believed that registering as an organ donor could potentially save someone's life. Approximately 507 individuals (81.91%) were willing to register as organ donors if their family had no objections to donating their organs upon their demise. Likewise, 537 participants (86.75%) expressed readiness to register as organ donors if provided with more information about organ transplantation procedures. Moreover, 531 participants (85.78%) showed willingness to register as organ donors if informed about their religion's perspective on organ donation, and 524 individuals (84.65%) expressed a similar intention if they knew where to register.

Within the group of participants who supported the opt-out consent system, 571 individuals (92.25%) believed that both living and posthumous organ donation would positively impact life after death. Additionally, 482 participants (77.87%) were of the opinion that support to the families of the deceased, regardless of their decision to donate organs, would increase organ donation rates. Conversely, only 207 participants (33.44%) strongly believed that doctors might not provide adequate care if the patient is a registered organ donor, while 176 (28.43%) expressed concerns that postmortem organ retrieval might lead to bodily disfigurement.

Furthermore, 282 individuals (45.56%) believed they were healthy enough to donate organs, and 357 (57.67%) felt their age was appropriate for donation. On the other hand, 315 participants (50.89%) feared that organ donation might leave them weakened or disabled. A smaller subset, 173 participants (27.95%), did not find the operation procedures for obtaining organs discouraging.

Concerns were also raised about the process of registering as an organ donor: 149 participants (24.07%) considered it time-consuming, and 139 (22.46%) expressed worry about the emotional distress their family members might experience during the organ retrieval process.

Regarding willingness to register as an organ or tissue donor among those who supported opt-out consent, 129 participants (20.84%) showed interest in registering, while 52 (8.4%) expressed disinterest, and 225 (36.34%) remained undecided.

There was statistically significant association between all TPB variables and opt-out consent for organ donation registration. These findings were summarised in the Table 2.

# Organ Donors: The Case for an Opt-Out System

## Could we be saving lives if we restructured our organ donation system?

![Aaron Schnoor](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*uh-N6vxeHMvFHMftWJCXaw.jpeg)

2

Share

![]()

In an age of limited supply and higher prices, there is perhaps no commodity that is more limited and valued than an organ.

I’m talking about human organs, of course. Or, more specifically, I’m talking about the need for human organs through transplants.

It’s no secret that human organs are in high demand.

According to the [United States government](https://www.organdonor.gov/learn/organ-donation-statistics), there are currently 104,234 men, women, and children on the national organ transplant list. 17 individuals die each day waiting for a new kidney, liver, pancreas, heart, lung, or other organ.

The number of individuals on the organ transplant waiting list has risen drastically over the past 30 years:

![]()

It’s a classic problem that economists have struggled with for decades: there’s a high demand for organs. There’s a limited supply.

And yet, perhaps the supply of organs is not as low as we think.

MED. 19 (2007). 22 Id. 23 REV. UNIF. ANATOMICAL GIFT ACT § 7 (UNIF. L. COMM’N 2006). 24 Id. 2024] TUG OF WAR BETWEEN OPT-IN AND OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS 23 form of communication that was made by the individual whilst they had a terminal illness.25 The UAGA of 2006 further provides that “in the absence of an express, contrary indication by the individual…an individual’s unrevoked refusal to make an anatomical gift bars all other persons from making an anatomical gift of the individual’s body.26 Thus, if an individual refuses to partake in organ donation, and that refusal goes unrevoked until their death, nothing and no one could take that individual’s organs upon their death for purposes of a transplant.27 Further, just because an individual consents to have one organ donated upon their death does not mean that they have consented to all of their organs being donated. III. OPT-IN VS. OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS A. GENERALLY Organ donation was introduced as a field of medicine geared towards saving lives; however, the manner in which a nation facilitates such an act may differ. Organ donation consists of two major systems: opt-out and opt-in systems. Opt-out organ donation systems presume that every individual is willing to donate their organs upon death; it allows for the removal of organs if there has been no prior objection made by the decedent.28 These types of systems are supported by two schools of thought: presumed consent and routine removal.29 Presumed consent is the idea that individuals are able to object to organ donation, and if they do not object whilst alive, their consent to donate their organs is presumed; this is based on a “presumed voluntariness to donate.”30 On the other hand, routine removal is more of an altruistic, rather than individualistic way of thinking. Routine removal consists of the idea that the State is allowed to have access to all organs of deceased persons. This line of reasoning acknowledges that individuals, especially 25 Id. 26 Id. 27 See id. 28 Leili Monfared & Lois Shepherd, Organ Procurement Now: Does the United States Still “Opt In?,” 2017 U. ILL. L. REV. 1003, 1011 (2017). 29 Id. 30 Id. DEPAUL JOURNAL OF HEALTH CARE LAW [Vol. 26.1 24 families of a decedent, may have an interest in maintaining the organs of a deceased individual; however, that interest is superseded by living persons who need an organ.31 The default in opt-out systems is that organ removal will occur.32 As aforementioned, opt-in organ donation systems do not presume that everyone is a donor; rather, such systems require individuals to expressly declare their intent in order to be able to donate their organs upon death. The main justification that underlies this particular system is that individuals should have a “right to determine what shall be done with his or her body following death.”33 This line of reasoning is largely an ethical justification that strives to put the wishes of a deceased person at the forefront. The default within opt-in systems is that organ removal will not occur.34 Although the United States has been labeled as an opt-in nation, scholars have argued about whether any nation is strictly an opt-in nation.35 Such arguments have been brought to the forefront with regard to the role of families in organ donation. B. THE ROLE OF THE FAMILY The role of a family in many different aspects of life can come into play in a variety of contexts. For example, when a child becomes sick at school, the child’s parents are called, and their options are explored. Similarly, when an individual passes away, the family is usually the one to take charge in facilitating a funeral and/or visitation hours. In the field of organ donation, the role of the family of the decedent seems to differ between opt-in and opt-out systems; however, they seemingly boil down to the same outcome. With opt-in systems, families are consulted if an individual has not completed any information regarding organ donation throughout their life.36 If a deceased individual has not deliberately opted-in to donating their organs prior to death, professionals will ask the decedent’s family about what course of action should be taken; the list is detailed in Section 2 31 Id. at 1014. 32 Govert den Hartogh, The Role of the Relatives in Opt-In Systems of Postmortal Organ Procurement, 15 MED., HEALTH CARE & PHIL. 195, 195 (2012). 33 Monfared & Shepherd, supra note 28, at 1009. 34 Hartogh, supra note 32. 35 Id. at 197. 36 Midtgaard & Albertsen, supra note 7, at 191. 2024] TUG OF WAR BETWEEN OPT-IN AND OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS 25 of the UAGA of 1968.37 Suppose an individual passed away while a resident of an opt-in nation, and they did not explicitly state that they did not want their organs donated. In this situation, the family would be legally allowed to permit the removal of the decedent’s organs without the express consent from the decedent.38 Such a system gives great power to the next of kin in the sense that they are able to assign a person’s organs to be donated even though that person has not expressly “opted-in.” With the role that the family may play within an opt-out system, the outcome can be very similar to that of an opt-in system. As aforementioned, the default in opt-out systems is that organ donation will occur.39 The only way that the default does not occur is if the prospective donor deliberately opts-out of the organ donation system, or if the family refuses to donate the decedent’s organs upon their death.40 It is apparent that both opt-out and opt-in systems include the family in some manner. It is also evident that both systems do not fully embrace the important aspect of consent since the family is able to decide whether the decedent’s organs should be donated if the decedent did not make a decision that was expressed prior to their death.41 This is one way in which opt-in nations may blur the lines for some individuals. It may be wholly plausible to believe that one’s organs will only be donated in an opt-in nation if the individual opts in; this is evident in the name itself. However, as illustrated above, the decedent’s family can make the ultimate decision if there is no record of “opting-in.” The family may donate the decedent’s organs, per their own choice, even if the decedent may have opposed that idea, but never outrightly expressed their opinions. This is a reason why candid conversations amongst family members are very important, especially regarding organ donation. However, even if the family decides to not donate the decedent’s organs, it seems as if consent still does not play a large part in the process; there may be no actual consent that was given by the decedent to donate their organs. 37 Hartogh, supra note 32, at 197. 38 Id. at 196. 39 Id. at 195. 40 Id. at 196. 41 Id. at 202. DEPAUL JOURNAL OF HEALTH CARE LAW [Vol. 26.1 26 IV. THE UNITED STATES – AN OPT-IN NATION The United States has labeled itself as an opt-in nation through the adoption of the UAGA in some states.42 This system puts first-person consent at the forefront with subsequent preference to the family; if consent from neither is presented, the default heeds to non-donation.43 Within the opt-in system in the United States, there are two ways in which individuals can opt-in; they may either sign themselves up through a valid donor registry, or they can indicate their intent to donate when they apply for a license or identification card from the Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV).44 Although these avenues may seem fairly straightforward, they do not tend to fully inform the individual. For example, there can be many necessary steps to take prior to transplanting an organ, and “…minimal, if any, information about them is made available…nor have efforts been made to implement broader public education regarding the use of these measures.”45 The aforementioned methods of opting-in may not fully inform a potential donor. The “campaign” of organ donation is focused on organs themselves; however, this may leave individuals uninformed that their tissues may also be donated.46 Organs that can be donated include: the heart; lungs; liver; kidney; stomach; and more.47 Tissues that can be donated include: the cornea; skin; tendons; heart valves; and more.48 This may manifest itself into a much larger issue because willingness to donate may differ for certain organs and tissues. A study revealed that, in the United States, 49% of adolescents disclosed that they were willing to donate their organs upon death.49 42 Monfared & Shepherd, supra note 28, at 1009–10. 43 Id. at 1011. 44 Id. at 1032. 45 Id. at 1033. 46 Id. 47 Alberta Health Services, Organ and Tissue Donation in Alberta, MYHEALTH.ALBERTA.CA (Mar. 31, 2020), https://myhealth.alberta.ca/alberta/Pages/organ-and-tissue-donation-topic-overview.aspx#:~:text=Organ%20donation%20is%20when%20an,and%20tr ansplanted%20into%20another%20person. 48 Id. 49 Nicola J. Williams & Neil C. Manson, ‘Take My Kidneys but Not My Corneas’—Selective Preferences as a Hidden Problem For ‘Opt-Out’ Organ Donation Policy, 36 BIOETHICS 829, 831 (2022). 2024] TUG OF WAR BETWEEN OPT-IN AND OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS 27 However, 33% of the 49% of willing donors said that they would restrict which organs and tissues could be donated; the eyes tend to be the organ that individuals were most likely to restrict from being able to be donated.50 Within opt-in systems, the lines become blurred when an individual has selective preference over which organs and tissues they would like to donate upon death; individuals may not fully embrace an “all in” mentality when approaching organ donation. If an individual opts out in an opt-out nation, the nation is able to determine the scope of consent if the individual did not opt out to donating all or particular organs and tissues.51 Failure to take such information and donor objections into account can skew the process of organ donation in a way that makes it seems as if the final result is all that matters. A. WHY IS THE UNITED STATES AN OPT-IN NATION? As previously mentioned, the United States is an opt-in nation; however, one may wonder if there would be any benefits if it moved towards an opt-out system. Although this may seem like an obvious decision in deciding between one of two options, there are certain ideals that engulf the United States’ opt-in system that may make an opt-in system more attractive. Currently, the United States is the nation that is leading in “donors per million and donors per deaths.”52 Many may wonder if this is a direct result of the opt-in system itself, or if there are other considerations that coincide with the system. When choosing between an opt-in and opt-out system, a nation must consider external factors. These are factors other than what the final outcome of organ donation will look like. A nation “must examine which system best aligns with their political-structure, culture, and legislative oversight.”53 With those factors in mind, the United States has reaffirmed the opt-in system through the revised UAGA of 2006. Although there were earlier versions of 50 Id. 51 Id. at 836. 52 The Alliance, Opt-In vs Opt-Out Donation Systems, 8 ALLIANCE SPOTLIGHT SERIES, no. 3, https://www.organdonationalliance.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Volume-VIII-Issue-3.pdf (last visited Jan. 28, 2024). 53 Id. DEPAUL JOURNAL OF HEALTH CARE LAW [Vol. 26.1 28 the UAGA, the UAGA of 2006 deliberately characterized the United States as an opt-in nation.54 While not abundantly clear what the exact reasoning behind characterizing the United States as an opt-in nation was, we do know that United States citizens “tend to value individual rights,” and “exhibit a more skeptical attitude towards the government.”55 With these factors in mind, it is plausible to believe that implementing an opt-in system that recognizes autonomy and individual choice would be the most sound option. 1. Attitudes Toward Individual Rights in the United States As previously mentioned, an opt-in system may value individual autonomy in that a person has to deliberately report that they would like to donate their organs; if they do not express an intent to donate, then their organs will most likely not be donated. There is a small caveat to this process; if a deceased individual did not explicitly register as an organ donor whilst they were alive, their family is consulted. The approach may differ if the decedent consented to donation, or if the decedent refused donation; in either case, such a family decision may “violate the right to control one’s body after death.”56 While it may be a common understanding that everyone should have the right to decide what happens to their body upon death, circumventing the decedent’s decision through their family may outrightly violate the decedent’s wishes. For example, if someone decides to donate their organs upon death, they provided their consent to an action “that would otherwise be a violation of bodily integrity.”57 Similarly, if someone does not decide to donate their organs in an opt-in nation, and their family decides to donate the decedent’s organs, this also violates the bodily integrity of that individual.58 It is apparent that although citizens of the United States may value individual rights more than other nations, the role of the family may heed against these rights. 54 See Verheijde et al., supra note 21. 55 The Alliance, supra note 52. 56 David Shaw et al., Family Overrule of Registered Refusal of Donate Organs, 21 J. INTENSIVE CARE SOC’Y 179, 181 (2020). 57 Id. 58 Id. 2024] TUG OF WAR BETWEEN OPT-IN AND OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS 29 It should come as no surprise that the United States was founded upon particular freedoms. Not only is this emphasized in the Constitution, but the Constitution itself “serves as a guide concerning the freedoms that [one] may enjoy as an individual.”59 When the Founding Fathers drafted the Constitution, they drafted it with an eye catered towards establishing a limited government. The thought was that this would sufficiently safeguard individual freedoms.60 However, they quickly realized that additions were needed to provide individuals with the rights and freedoms that they deserve as citizens. With this in mind, the Founding Fathers drafted a bill of rights to amend the Constitution, geared towards the rights of citizens. For example, the First Amendment of the Bill of Rights provides certain freedoms: The freedom of religion, speech, press, and the freedom to assemble and petition.61 Further, the Second Amendment allows citizens to “keep and bear Arms.”62 There are more freedoms and rights listed in the Bill of Rights, but they all collectively heed to the fact that the founding fathers recognized that citizens deserved additional rights not expressed in the original Constitution. This all leads to a particular question; if the United States values individual rights and freedoms in other areas of an individual’s life, why should the United States not allow individuals to withhold their consent to opt-in to organ donation? Perhaps this is a reason why the United States has deployed the opt-in system; in order for an individual to donate their organs, they must expressly declare their intent to do so. While the United States may value individual rights more than other nations, Americans themselves are not convinced that their liberties and rights will be protected in the future.63 A research study conducted by the APM Research Lab revealed that 60% of the Americans in their data set believed that they 59 Christopher Brown, How Does The Constitution Protect My Individual Rights?, THE BROWN FIRM (Mar. 21, 2022), https://www.brownfirmpllc.com/how-does-the-constitution-protect-my-individual-rights/. 60 Id. 61 U.S. CONST. amend. I. 62 U.S. CONST. amend II. 63 Emily Schmidt et al., Poll: Most Americans Think Liberty Has Waned, Rights Will Further Diminish, APM RESEARCH LAB (June 30, 2022), https://www.apmresearchlab.org/motn/what-americans-think-about-liberty-rights-freedom-may-2022. DEPAUL JOURNAL OF HEALTH CARE LAW [Vol. 26.1 30 will see a “net loss of liberties, freedom and rights over the next decade.”64 Even though there have been major developments in areas of marriage equality, use of recreational marijuana, etc., citizens on both sides of the political spectrum still “feel that fundamental rights are at risk.”65 This may make proponents of opt-in systems reconsider their stance; since Americans believe that their rights will be at risk over the next decade, how will an opt-in system generate more organ donations if such individuals do not have faith that their rights will be respected? 2. Distrust in the Government of the United States As mentioned previously, in addition to the United States tending to value individual rights more than other nations, Americans also exhibit the expression that they may not trust their government.66 This factor may lead to a nation choosing an opt-in system because in the end, it is the individual that makes the choice to be an organ donor; in other words, the government cannot force an individual to be an organ donor when they, for example, present themselves at the DMV. This line of reasoning is understandable; if an individual does not trust the government, they most likely would not want a system in place where the government makes decisions for them, especially decisions relating to them being an organ donor. A Pew Research Center study from 2024 demonstrated that less than two out of ten Americans trust the government to “do what is right” either “just about always” or “most of the time.”67 This is not a trend that was seen at, and immediately after, the founding of the United States. In fact, data demonstrated that these were the lowest measures of trust that had been reported in nearly seven decades.68 Public trust in the government began to fall in the 1960s due to the Vietnam War, and decreased further with the Watergate scandal.69 However, nearly thirty years later, public trust of the government was at an all-time high; this was 64 Id. 65 Id. 66 The Alliance, supra note 52. 67 Public Trust in Government: 1958-2023, PEW RSCH. CTR. (June 24, 2024), https://www.pewresearch.org/politics/2023/09/19/public-trust-in-government-1958-2023/. 68 Id. 69 Id. 2024] TUG OF WAR BETWEEN OPT-IN AND OPT-OUT ORGAN DONATION SYSTEMS 31 due to the United States’ response to the terrorist attacks that occurred on September 11, 2001.70 Despite current trust in the government being at an all-time low, the opt-in system still functions rather well because the government ultimately cannot decide for an individual that they should ‘opt-in’ to being an organ donor. V. OPT-OUT SYSTEMS IN ACTION AROUND THE WORLD While the United States currently facilitates an opt-in system, nations such as Spain, Austria, France, and the United Kingdom deploy opt-out systems.71 However, nations that have opt-out systems do not tend to function as a wholly opt-out system; this can be seen through the system used in both Spain and Singapore. A. SPAIN: An Untraditional ‘Opt-Out’ Nation Spain is typically regarded as a “success story” in the realm of organ donation; it has the “highest number of deceased donors PMP (per million population) internationally.72 In 2019, Spain had 49.6% donors PMP, while the United States had 36.8% donors PMP.73 It is plausible to wonder whether Spain’s success story stems directly from its use of an opt-out system. However, Spanish transplant experts elaborated that they do not “consider Spain to be an ‘opt-out’ country.”74 Traditionally, an opt-out nation would typically have some sort of registry where an individual can “opt-out,” and express their desire of not wanting to be an organ donor; however, Spain does not have such a system in place.75 In addition to not facilitating an official opt-out registry, families of a deceased individual will always be approached in order to authorize organ donation; these factors tend to directly contradict what would traditionally make up an opt-out system.76 70 Id. 71 The Alliance, supra note 52. 72 Etheredge, supra note 3, at 1989. 73 Id. 74 Id. 75 Id. 76 Id. enriched_points.expanded_content: Countries like Wales and Scotland implemented opt-out systems, presuming consent for organ donation unless individuals actively decline, significantly streamlining the donation process.
Studies indicate that opt-out systems can lead to a 17-56% increase in willingness to donate organs, translating into higher donor rates compared to opt-in systems.
Behavioral insights highlight the importance of public awareness and family discussions on organ donation, enhancing societal support and increasing family authorization rates for donations.
Successful opt-out implementations stress the need for continuous public education and addressing cultural attitudes, ensuring that opt-out systems effectively increase organ donation rates.
